coredata.dc:title: Simple detection of gluten in wheat-containing food samples of celiac diets with a novel fluorescent nanosensor made of folic acid-based carbon dots through molecularly imprinted technique
coredata.dc:creator: Karamdoust, Sanaz
Milani-Hosseini, Mohammad-Reza
Faridbod, Farnoush
coredata.dc:description: A nanosensor is designed for rapid detection of the gluten content of wheat-containing samples. Gluten is a plant protein that causes allergy in individuals and leads to celiac disease. Since in a celiac diet trace amounts of gluten are able to prompt allergic reactions, a food-allergen label must be provided on foodstuffs and be seriously considered by food industries. Various analytical methods and commercial immunoassays are used for such analyses but prices per test, especially for low-income countries are high. Thus, a rapid, sensitive, simple, and inexpensive detecting tool seems essential. A solution can be designing a gluten optical nanosensor. The nanosensor is made of folic-acid-carbon dots and gluten molecularly templates embedded simultaneously in a silicate matrix. Adding gluten to the solution of this nanostructure and its adsorbing on the blank templated space on the nanostructure causes fluorescence enhancement. The concentration range of gluten detection was 0.36 to 2.20 µM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gluten
Nanosensor
Fluorescent detection
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Bread samples
Folic acid carbon dots

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the FA-CDs as a fluorescent probe
Preparation of the FA-CDs-MIP as a nanosensor
Fluorescent measurements of gluten by the designed nanosensor
Bread and flour gluten content determination by standard addition method
Characterization of CDs, CDs-MIP and CDs-NIP
Response mechanism of the fluorescent nanosensor
Optimization of parameters affecting the performance of nano-sensor
Gluten detection by the FA- CDs-MIP nanosensor
Study of repeatability, reproducibility, and stability of designed nanosensor
Analysis of gluten in bread and flour samples
Comparing the performance of the proposed nanosensor with previously reported optical methods for gluten detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Iran University of Science and Technology
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Iran University of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The research explained in the article was financially supported by Iran University of Science and Technology and University of Tehran.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CDs; TEM image (A), UV–vis spectrum (B) Fluorescence spectrum (C), FT-IR (D), and XRD (E).

FT-IR spectrum of CDs (black), CDs-APTES (orange) and CDs-MIP (green) (A), TEM images of CDs-MIP (B) and CDs-NIP (C), SEM images of CDs-MIP (D) and CDs-NIP (E). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Effect of pH (A) and effect of incubation time (B) on the fluorescence enhancing of CDs-MIP; Fluorescence spectra of CDs-MIP(C)and CDs-NIP (D) in the presence of different concentrations of gluten and calibration curves.
Schematic of FA-CDs-MIP preparation steps and gluten detection.

head.ce:title: Simple detection of gluten in wheat-containing food samples of celiac diets with a novel fluorescent nanosensor made of folic acid-based carbon dots through molecularly imprinted technique

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Laboratory of Real Samples Analysis, Faculty of Chemistry, Iran University of Science and Technology, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Real Samples Analysis
Iran University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Laboratory of Real Samples Analysis, Faculty of Chemistry, Iran University of Science and Technology, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Excellence in Electrochemistry, Analytical Chemistry Department, School of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center of Excellence in Electrochemistry, Analytical Chemistry Department, School of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A nanosensor is designed for rapid detection of the gluten content of wheat-containing samples. Gluten is a plant protein that causes allergy in individuals and leads to celiac disease. Since in a celiac diet trace amounts of gluten are able to prompt allergic reactions, a food-allergen label must be provided on foodstuffs and be seriously considered by food industries. Various analytical methods and commercial immunoassays are used for such analyses but prices per test, especially for low-income countries are high. Thus, a rapid, sensitive, simple, and inexpensive detecting tool seems essential. A solution can be designing a gluten optical nanosensor. The nanosensor is made of folic-acid-carbon dots and gluten molecularly templates embedded simultaneously in a silicate matrix. Adding gluten to the solution of this nanostructure and its adsorbing on the blank templated space on the nanostructure causes fluorescence enhancement. The concentration range of gluten detection was 0.36 to 2.20 µM.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Food allergies are an evolving issue that has recently affected public health and may become life-threatening for adults and children (Xhaferaj, Alves, Ferreira, & Scherf, 2020). Worldwide, there are several food groups causing allergies in humans. Among them, wheat can be considered one of the most vital ones because of the gluten content inside it (Yu et al., 2021). Gluten, in fact, is a structural protein in grains such as wheat, barley, rye, and some grains. It is the most common and known food allergen. It is also used as a binder, emulsifier, or flavor in food industries (Wieser, Segura, Ruiz-Carnicer, Sousa, & Comino, 2021). In people who have gluten-related disorders, called celiac disease (CD), consuming the gluten content products, is triggered an immunological reaction as a result of abnormal responses of the immune system toward gluten intolerance (Osorio, Mejías, & Rustgi, 2019). CD is one of the most hypersensitive autoimmune disorders that affects roughly 1 % of the general population worldwide and principally is caused by a combination of genetic and environmental factors (Chaudhry, Jacobs, Green, & Rampertab, 2021).

Although avoidance of gluten and following a strict diet might alleviate celiac disease symptoms; completely eliminating it from the daily diet would become challenging (Falcomer et al., 2020). To manage this food intolerance and to protect the health of CD patients from serious hazardous effects, the use of the label “gluten-free” for food products has been recommended. Nowadays, to ensure consumer safety, many countries have adopted food-labeling requirements in food industries. It should be noted that the observation of “gluten-free” labels on a food product in markets does not exactly mean that the nutriment is completely free of gluten (Coto, Mendia, Sousa, Bai, & Cebolla, 2021). To address this issue, regulatory authorities have accepted a threshold level of 20 mg/kg limit of gluten as a “gluten-free” label (CODEX STAN118‐1979).

Assurance of the correct implementation of gluten-free labeling depends on the accuracy and limitations of the analytical methods used for quantifying the gluten residue (Wieser et al., 2021). Over the years, different detection techniques have been reported to analyze gluten protein. They include electrochemical analytical methods (Malvano, Albanese, Pilloton, & Di Matteo, 2017; Svigelj, Zuliani, Grazioli, Dossi, & Toniolo, 2022), liquid chromatography (Lexhaller, Colgrave, & Scherf, 2019), mass spectrometry (MS) (Lu, Ji, Chen, Li, & Timira, 2022), and immunological and genomic assays (Yu et al., 2021). Among these methods, liquid chromatography although providing reliable and sensitive results, still in some reports suffers the drawback of being unable to distinguish between gluten and non-gluten proteins in the food samples. MS can be used for the quantification of gluten by analyzing the nature of the proteins in diets (Cao, Baumert, & Downs, 2021). However, significant limitation such as insufficient accuracy in the analysis of complex hydrolyzed samples, and especially the high expense of the method has directed to develop new alternatives (Su, Liang, & Tan, 2021). All these mentioned techniques are also time-consuming and need expert operators. Besides these techniques, there are some commercial methods too. Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) test kits and polymerase chain reaction (PCR) based assays are applicable in routine quality controls. PCR, as genomic methods in particular, relies on the principle of deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) sequence detection. ELISA test kits, as immunological assays, are based on antibodies raised against specific amino acid sequences. Despite the sensitive, simple, fast response, and inexpensive equipment, these two approaches are not affordable by all consumers. They are used some biological receptors such as monoclonal antibodies which causes the kits to become priceable for low-income countries. Also, these biomolecules should be labeled with some organic dyes which technologies is monopolized by several developed countries. Furthermore, in heat-processed food samples, antibodies lose reactivity and prevent to detection of gluten in food samples accurately. These limitations cause wide applications of these tools to be limited in food industries.

To compensate for these deficiencies, new analytical methods such as chemical sensors/biosensors have become a trend (Gupta, Kaushal, Kumar, & Kumar, 2019). Although some researchers tried to replace antibodies with DNA aptamers in their design (Malvano et al., 2017; Svigelj et al., 2022), some others still used antibodies as a bioreceptor in a lateral flow immunochromatographic test assay (Hnasko, Jackson, Lin, Haff, & McGarvey, 2021; Momeni et al., 2022). Maybe one of the remarkable designed biosensor-based antibodies is a portable gluten sensor which showed a positive or negative qualitative response (Zhang et al., 2019). Development of nanomaterials in has leaded to designing new sensors with improved analytical performances. In this regards, optical nanomaterials were predominant. In two recent reports, gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) encapsulated in the surface of crystalline nanocellulose were used to detect gluten in bread samples (Mousavizadeh & Sarlak 2022, 2023). In most cases, bioreceptors or valuable or complex nanomaterials were utilized in designing gluten sensors. If ones want to eliminate the natural bioreceptor from the design, and just use some complex composites of nanomaterials, the selectivity of the sensor will be affected. This issue is very important in the analysis of the complex matrix of real samples.

In this work, to overcome the elimination effect of antibody or aptamer, molecularly imprinted biomimetic technology was employed. A literature survey shows that there is only one report on using gluten molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) which is an electrochemical sensor (Limthin et al., 2019). In that work, Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles were prepared through a chemical reaction and then encapsulated with methyl methacrylate (MMA) as a functional group. MMA is an organic monomer and may not be suitable for an optical design. However, here, inorganic silicate monomers with optical transparency and low toxicity were used. The proposed nanosensor is made of fluorescent nanomaterials and gluten molecular templates that are simultaneously embedded in a silicate matrix.

Among the wide range of optical nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) are one of the promising carbon nanostructures (Kaur & Aulakh, 2021) because of their unique physical and chemical stability, low toxicity, and facile synthesis procedure (Ross, Wu, Wei, Ross, & Chang, 2020). Also, outstanding water solubility, excellent photo-physical characteristics and large-scale preparation (Liu, Li, & Yang, 2020) are the other noticeable features. Hence, they can be selected as an excellent choice for fabricating fluorescent probes (Wang, Feng, Dong, & Huang, 2019). The selection of a suitable carbon source is a very significant issue for the fabrication of high-quality carbon quantum dots. One effective method to enhance the electronic and optical properties, as fluorescence quantum yield (QY), is the passivation or functionalization of carbon quantum dots surface (Khayal et al., 2021). During the surface modification, some active sites such as hydroxyl, amine, and carboxyl groups are inserted on the carbon quantum dot surface for functional modification purposes (Mishra, Patil, Thakur, & Kesharwani, 2018). However, some precursors due to consisting of heteroatoms in their structures do not require any further surface modification and act as doped carbon quantum dots (Miao et al., 2020). Here, carbon dots were synthesized from N-(4-{[(2-amino-4-oxo-1,4-dihydropteridin-6-yl)methyl]amino}benzoyl)-l-glutamic acid (called folic acid (FA) or Vitamine B9), as a single precursor in a one-step approach. FA was chosen here as a single precursor to produce N-doped carbon quantum dots without adding any additives or passivation agents (Babanyinah, 2021; Liu, Cao, Gui, & Ma, 2018).

As a conclusion, in this work, a nanosensor was designed and introduce for rapid, selective, inexpensive, and simple detection of the gluten content of wheat-containing samples. The proposed nanosensor is made of a fluorescent nanomaterial and gluten molecular imprinted template that are simultaneously embedded in a silicate matrix. The nanomaterial, folic acid-based-carbon dots (FA-CDs), was prepared easily and used as a fluorescent probe. The final synthesized nanostructure was dispersed in a suitable buffer and was used as an optical kit in the detection of gluten. After the addition of gluten to this solution, the fluorescence of the structure enhanced effectively due to the adsorption of gluten molecules on the templated space of the nanostructure. Finally, the designed optical nanosensor was successfully applied for the detection of the gluten content of different kinds of wheat flour, some bread samples containing gluten and others labeled as gluten-free.

Folic acid (FA), N-(4-{[(2-amino-4-oxo-1,4-dihydropteridin-6-yl)methyl]amino}benzoyl)-l-glutamic acid (with a commercial name of Vitamin B9), as a precursor, has been purchased from a local pharmaceutical factory in Iran. Other reagents with analytical grade such as.

3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), Tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), ethanol, hydrochloric acid, sodium hydroxide, trisodium phosphate (Na3PO4), and ammonium hydroxide and ammonium fluoride were all obtained from Merck Chemical Co. Throughout the experiment, double-distilled water and phosphate buffer saline (PBS) were used for the preparation of all solutions.

High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, Leo 906, Zeiss, Germany) images and scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi Model 450 electron micro-scope, Japan) images were obtained to characterize the morphology of the nanostructure. Fluorescence spectra were obtained using Perkin-Elmer (LS50) luminescence spectrometer. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) were carried out on a Perkin-Elmer Fourier transform infrared spectrometer, and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) mapping was measured by MIRA III (SAMX detector).

Gluten is a water-insoluble protein and is classified into two fractions, glutenin and gliadin. Gliadin is an alcohol soluble component, and glutenin is its acid/alkali soluble ingredient. In order to prepare standard gluten solution 0.01 g gluten was dissolved in 2 mL 60 % ethanol. The stock solution was leftover at 4 °C for further usage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the FA-CDs as a fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental procedure to prepare FA-CD is explained by the following procedure. Initially, 0.1 g of FA was dissolved in 5 mL of water. Later, 0.1 g of trisodium phosphate was added to the solution and dissolved by stirring. Afterwards, the solution was put in a beaker heated at ∼9–120 °C for about 2 h. During the procedure, the mixture color progressively altered from amber yellow to mustard yellow. At 12,000 rpm for 20 min, the obtained mustard yellow solution was centrifuged to remove the agglomerated particles and the blend was dialyzed using a dialysis membrane. The resulting FA-CD was used as a stock solution for further testing. At this stage, the prepared FA-CDs were characterized by HR-TEM, ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrum, fluorescence spectroscopy, and FTIR.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the FA-CDs-MIP as a nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: After synthesizing FA-CDs, the procedure was continued to prepare FA-CDs molecularly imprinted polymer (FA-CDs-MIP) as the nanosensor. First, 2 mL APTES, as a monomer was added to the prepared nanoparticles FA-CDs solution. Next, the mixture was heated at 35 °C for about 40 min by stirring with a magnate until a viscous compound was obtained. After cooling the blend to room temperature, 2 mL of the prepared gluten solution was mixed with APTES- FA-CD. Then, the process was finished by adding 2 mL of TEOS as a crosslinker, plus a catalyst solution of 2 g NH4F, 100 mL H2O, and 23 mL NH4OH.

After spending 24 h to rest, the final synthesized FA-CDs-MIP was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min. Later, to remove gluten as the template molecule and the extra materials, the FA-CD-MIP nanomaterial was washed several times by deionized water and ethanol mixture. Through the washing process, the same solvent used for the analyte was added to the prepared polymer then centrifuged. Afterwards, at every stage, supernatant solution was analyzed by UV–vis to certify the removal of gluten molecule by no evidence of gluten peak in the UV–vis region. In addition, the same process was taken for synthesizing FA-CDs none-imprinting polymer (FA-CDs-NIP), in which the gluten molecule is absence during the procedure. It was used as a control material for FA-CDs-MIP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent measurements of gluten by the designed nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: A general procedure for the gluten measurement by the nanosensor is as follow: 0.1 g of FA-CDs-MIP dried powder was dispersed in PBS buffer in a 100 mL-volumetric flask, and sonicated for 2 h. After this time, 3 mL of this solution was moved to a 4.5 mL-fluorescent quartz cuvette and the fluorescence signal of the solution is recorded (F0). Then, 10 μL of the gluten containing solution was injected into FA-CDs-MIP solution and then again fluorescent signal was recorded (F). By addition of gluten solution to the probe solution, gluten molecules place on the vacant templated space in the nanostructure which leads to the fluorescence enhancement. The ratio of these two fluorescence intensity signals is considered as the nanosensor signal (F/F0). Later, for supplementary experiments, the extra FA-CDs-MIP solution was stored at 4 °C.

The numerous cereals and processed foods containing gluten, bread can be selected to harbor the highest percentage of gluten. The reason is that the main ingredient is wheat flour, which naturally consists gluten. Therefore, in this study for real sample test, four different wheat flours and Lavash-breads (a kind of Lavash-bread in Iran) was chosen as they are included in the daily human diet. Besides, to complete the experiment, gluten free (GF) wheat flour and Lavash-bread samples were also purchased.

All wheat flours and Lavash-breads containing gluten samples were processed following the instruction of Veratox® for Gliadin R5 ELISA commercial kit (Yu et al., 2021). Briefly, 1 g, of the flour was weighed. Then, 10 mL of 60 % ethanol was added to the test tube and vortex for 10 min for extracting gluten. The extract was centrifuging for 5 min at 2500 rpm, then, 100 μL of the supernatant, was diluted to 4.9 mL with PBS. Later, the mixture was vortexed again for 5 s to have a homogeneousness solution. Subsequently, the sample for each wheat flour, was utilized for gluten analysis through standard addition method. The same procedure was repeated for the other bread samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bread and flour gluten content determination by standard addition method
ce:section.ce:para: For performing standard addition method, a series of calibration solutions are needed. A certain volume (5 mL) of an unknown concentration of the solution of every prepared real sample was added to each 25-mL volumetric flask and a series of increasing volumes of 0, 5, 10, 15, 20 mL of a gluten standard solution was added to the volumetric flasks. Then, through standard addition calibration curve, the concentration of the unknown gluten was calculated.

The proposed gluten measurement kit is a novel chemical MIP based fluorescent nanosensor. The fluorescent probe is composed of synthesized folic acid based-carbon dots (FA-CDs) embedded in a silicate matrix. FA, a small organic molecule consisting of amino, carboxyl and, hydroxyl groups as well as a benzene ring can be a suitable candidate to generate carbon dots with high optical properties (Fahmi et al., 2021; Lin, Ding, Zhang, & Huang, 2018). This small organic molecule also demonstrates excellent photostability in wide pH range solutions (Li, Wang, Li, Zhu, & Liu, 2018).

As well as selection of suitable carbon source, to improve the selectivity of the nanosensor, molecular imprinted technology was used (Fang et al., 2021). Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) are imprinted polymers which are synthesize through polymerization of functional monomers around a template molecule to produce artificial recognition sites that match the template. Recognition cavities are produced within the polymer structure after removing the template molecule.
Here, inorganic silicate monomers, APTES and cross-linker TEOS were used to form the matrix around the FA-CDs and gluten template (Zoughi, Faridbod, Amiri, & Ganjali, 2021).

FA-CDs, first modified with APTES, to form fluorescent monomers. Then, this modified monomers in presence of gluten molecules are polymerized with TEOS as cross-linker. Finally, gluten molecules removed from the prepared structure by washing. In this way, a silicate structure in which FA-CDs and vacant gluten-templated spaces are embedded, forms called FA-CDs-MIP (Scheme 1 ). By adding gluten solution to the FA-CDs-MIP dispersed solution, gluten molecules place on the vacant templated space in the structure which leads to the fluorescence enhancement. Following, the characterization of the prepared materials and the response mechanism of the designed nanosensor is considered.

In comparison to the traditional organic MIPs which methacrylic acid monomers were used, the silicate based MIPs have a benefits of optical transparency which is suitable for the fluorescent detection (Ahmad, Bedwell, Esen, Garcia-Cruz, & Piletsky, 2019).

During the past years, several fluorescent nanosensors based on carbon dots and MIP have been reported for the detection of different analytes in variety of applications (Díaz-Álvarez & Martín-Esteban, 2021). For instance, in food industry, it has been studied for the detection of tartrazine in fake saffron containing products (Zoughi et al., 2021). Also, in pharmaceutical samples, for example, this technique has been applied to detect entacapone for Parkinson’s disease (Ahmadi, Faridbod, & Mehrzad-Samarin, 2019). Moreover, in another study, the molecularly imprinted polymer based on carbon dots (CDs) synthesized by citric acid has been utilized as a nanosensor for antibiotic detection in bacterial toxic materials (Amiri, Faridbod, & Zoughi, 2021). Further on, for the treatment of inflammatory bowel diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis, sulfasalazine detection was obtained by the application of Molecularly Imprinted sensing film-based optical sensor (Motaharian, Hajebrahimi, Hosseini, & Khosrokhavar, 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs, CDs-MIP and CDs-NIP
ce:section.ce:para: HR-TEM microscopy was used to characterize the size of the synthesized FA-CDs nanoparticles. Size of the FA-CDs nanoparticles was smaller than 20 nm according to HR-TEM image (Fig. 1 A). In addition, the FA-CDs forming was also confirmed by a broad absorption edge peak from 320 nm to 390 nm in UV–vis spectrum. Fluorescence spectrum, verifying a symmetric fluorescence emission at 430 nm with excitation at 350 nm (Fig. 1B and C). Regarding the FT-IR analysis for carbon dots, two obvious peaks at 1636.59 cm−1 and 3300–3500 cm−1 prove the existence of carboxyl and hydroxyl functional groups, respectively. These peaks suggest the presence of carbonyl and hydroxyl functional groups on the surface and edges of CDs (Fig. 1D). Fig. 1E shows the XRD pattern for FA-CD corresponding to the crystal lattice of the prepared carbon nanoparticle. Similar characteristics previously was seen for the folic acid based carbon dots, although our synthesis producer is slightly varied (Fahmi et al., 2021; Li et al., 2018; Lin et al., 2018).

Furthermore, to examine the interactions between prepared FA-CDs and APTES, an FT-IR spectra was taken (Fig. 2 A). The presence of carbonyl groups can be confirmed by the peak at 1636.59 cm−1 in Fig. 2A (black). Also, a broadband from 3000 to 3500 cm−1 shows the presence of the hydroxyl groups for FA-CD (Fig. 2A black). Besides, the observed oxygen functional groups on the surface of FA-CD contribute to the water solubility property, which means that the presence of hydrophilic groups in FA-CD makes it easy to dissolve in water.

Moreover, the investigation via FT-IR spectrum, indicates the presence of CN, CN, CN-C on the surface and that the prepared FA-CD consists of three main elements as, C, O and N. Also, the existence of amino group on the resulting carbon dot surface, exhibits sustainable florescence property.

Additionally, in the FT-IR spectrum of FA-CDs-APTES (Fig. 2A orange), covalent SiOC interactions have been strongly indicated by a peak around 997.69 cm−1. Moreover, the appearance of a peak at 3357.39 cm−1 is due to the amine group in APTES. The peaks at 3200 and 3450 cm−1 indicate the NH bond stretching related to the existing amino group in APTES. Shift in the carbonyl peak from 1638.115 cm−1 to 1600 cm−1, can be because of the formation of NH3 + and COO– of amine groups of APTES and the carboxylic groups of FA-CDs (Ahmadi et al., 2019).

Moreover, Fig. 2A orange demonstrates many free amino groups from ATPES which can interact with functional groups of gluten. Also, taking into concern the FTIR spectrum peaks for FA-CDs-MIP (Fig. 2A green), it can be concluded that the interaction is stronger than those for FA-CDs-APTES; thus, this confirms that the polymerization is completed.

In order to find more detail about the structure of FA-CDs-MIP, and its control nanomaterial the FA-CDs-NIP, HR-TEM technique was utilized. Referring to HR-TEM images (Fig. 2B and C), the FA-CDs-MIP particles indicate lighter color in comparison with FA-CDs-NIP, which is due to the existence of some vacant spaces on its structure. Also, to study the morphology of FA-CDs-MIP and the FA-CDs-NIP, the SEM technique was used. According to the SEM images, the morphology of FA-CDs-MIP was confirmed to be sphere-shaped (Fig. 2D). Furthermore, because of the imprinted cavities exist on FA-CDs-MIP, the roughness of the material and spongy shape has been enlarged. On the other hand, in FA-CDs-NIP SEM images (Fig. 2E), the particles are denser and the surface is smoother. Supplementary experiments as EDX mapping were also done to find some details about the homogeneity of the synthesized material. As can be observed through (Fig. 1S). As can be observed through (Fig. 1S), particles are dispersed well in the FA-CDs- MIP nanocomposite. Similar behavior was observed in our previous researches (Ahmadi et al., 2019; Ahmadi et al., 2019; Zoughi et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response mechanism of the fluorescent nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: To understand the response mechanism of the proposed fluorescent nanosensor, it would be better the chemical structures of the nanomaterial and the gluten molecule and their chemical interaction to be considered. As it can be seen in Scheme 1, FA-CDs having enormous heteroatoms of N and O and functional groups, can easily react with amino groups of APTES molecules. Besides, because of using excess amounts of APTES, another layer of APTES was formed around the modified FA-CDs, which causes these fluorescent monomers to react with gluten as a template molecule. Then, using TEOS as a cross-linker, polymerization occurs and a silicate structure in which FA-CDs and gluten molecules embedded in it forms. It is noticeable that the interaction between gluten molecule and amine groups of the APTES layer is a hydrogen bonding. Hence, by simple washing, gluten molecules are removed from the nanostructure and empty places remaining. At this stage, the remaining compound is called FA-CDs-MIP and by dispersion of this nanostructure in PBS, a solution is prepared which can be used as a sensing kit. When gluten as the analyte is added to the sensing probe, it is adsorbed in the functional templated cavities which causes fluorescence enhancement of FA-CDs-MIP. In absence of gluten, the ion pairs on heteroatoms in the cavities are free. By adsorption of gluten on the empty spaces, and hydrogen bonding occur, it can be assumed that a photoinduced electron transfer may be inhibited and fluorescence emission enhances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of parameters affecting the performance of nano-sensor

ce:section.ce:para: To study the effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of FA-CDs-MIP nanocomposite, pH value tuned in the range of 5.0–9.0 (Fig. 3 A). Accordingly, a solution was prepared by 0.1 g FA-CDs- MIP dispersing in 100 mL Briton-Robinson buffer for two hours and the gluten concentration remained constant during the measurements. In the selected pH range, the response intensity changed. In both acidic (pH values lower than 7.0) and basic (pH values higher than 8.0) solutions the fluorescence enhancing of the nanosensor by gluten addition compared to neutral pH (7.0) solution was less. In the pH value above 7, ionization of the silicate layer may gradually begin which can cause some surface defects. Also, in the pH value lower than 7 due to the protonation of the N and O atoms of the binding sites on FA-CDs-MIP the sensitivity decreases. Therefore, neutral pH (7.0) was selected as the optimum pH for all measurements.

The time for maximum adsorption of gluten molecules on the FA-CDs-MIP nanosensor was also optimized (Fig. 3B). For this purpose, all other effective parameters such as pH, temperature, and gluten concentration were kept constant. Then, the time was set over the range of 1 to 6 min and the florescence of the FA-CDs-MIP nanosensor was recorded immediately after addition of gluten. The maximum fluorescence enhancement occurred after 2 min. Thus, this value was selected as the optimized incubation time in the analysis of gluten.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gluten detection by the FA- CDs-MIP nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: In the optimized conditions, the fluorescent intensities of FA-CDs@MIP and FA-CDs@ NIP, were studied in the different gluten concentrations as standard calibration series (Fig. 3C and D). The provided FA-CDs-MIP illustrated a notable response in a linear range from 0.36 µM to 2.20 µM and LOD as 0.10 µM. As seen in Fig. 4E, the determination coefficient (R2) was obtained 0.995 and 0.903 for FA-CDs-MIP and FA-CDs-NIP, respectively. Also, with comparison of the slope of the calibration curves for FA-CDs-MIP and FA-CDs-NIP, a higher sensitivity in first one is obviously seen. Considering the low LOD, the introduced optical nanosensor was effective and sensitive enough for analyzing trace amounts of gluten. The measurement of each concentration of gluten were done five times, and the relative standard deviation was measured as 2.2 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of repeatability, reproducibility, and stability of designed nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the repeatability of the proposed technique, a 1 μM gluten solution as a test solution was determined for five times by the optical nanosensor and the RSD was measured to be 2.1 %. Furthermore, to examine the reproducibility, five synthesized nanosensors were prepared and the gluten concentration (1 μM) as test solution was measured for each of them, and the RSD of all measurements was determined to be 2.4 %. Also, the stability of the materials (FA-CDs-MIP) used in the proposed optical nanosensor was studied by recording the fluorescence emission of the FA-CD-MIP for the duration five months. At the first month, and after three months, RSDs were calculated as 2.3 %, and 3.2 %. Finally at month six, relative standard deviation was measured to be 4.3 %. Typically, how the nanocomposite powder was stored, affects the result of the study. So, if the CDs-MIP powder is stored in the refrigerator in a dry place without being in contact with air, the nanocomposite can be stable for up to six months.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of gluten in bread and flour samples
ce:section.ce:para: To exploit practical application of FA-CDs-MIP as optical probes, four different types of gluten containing wheat flour and bread samples were analyzed. Moreover, to assess the validity for the nanosensor performance, a standard and certain concentration of gluten was added manually to a selected gluten free (GF) wheat flour and bread samples. The recovered concentrations of the spiked samples using the proposed nanosensor was acceptable (RSD % less than 4.3 % for five replicate measurements).

In this study, the gluten weight percentages (W/W %) was analyzed in the range of (8.3–8.9) % for all the wheat flours Also for plain blead the gluten was measured in the range of (25.4–33.1) g/kg. Thus, according to the obtained results, the current fluorescent probe is reliable for detecting gluten in pieces of bread Further on, for validation, the results of the fluorescent nanoprobe, was compared by RIDASCREEN, and the Veratox test kits, two of the AACC certified and AOAC approved kits for gluten analysis. For RIDASCREEN test kit the gluten concentration in wheat flours was measured as 86.9 ± 2.0 and 40.2 ± 0.1 g/kg for bread samples. Further on, for Veratox test kit, the result for plain breads and wheat flours were determined as 27.6 ± 0.4 and 51.2 ± 6.1 respectively (Yu et al., 2021). Thus, the present results for FA-CDs-MIP nanosensor are comparable with the mentioned kits and there is no noteworthy difference. Therefore, it can be mentioned that the current method is reliable. Also, referring to the 95 % confidence level by the statistical t-test, there wasn’t any significant alteration between the proposed nanosensor results and ones obtained from two-commercial test kit.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparing the performance of the proposed nanosensor with previously reported optical methods for gluten detection
ce:section.ce:para: Comparison of the linearity and detection limit of the nanosensor with two of the ELSA kits, RIDASCREEN® (linear range 5–80 mg/kg and detection limit 2.7 mg/kg), and Veratox® for R5 (linear range 5–80 mg/kg and detection limit 5 mg/kg) (Yu et al., 2021), was done. The proposed nanosensor like other kits is able to detect gluten under the tolerable limit of 20 mg/kg rapidly (linear range 15–92.4 mg/kg with detection limit 4.2 mg/kg). Thus, the proposed nanosensor kit can be utilized for trace analysis of gluten. Also, the required time for doing each test is an important factor in a detection method especially in a rapid test. The required time in available commercial Elisa kits is about maximum 90 min to minimum 4 min. Hence, the response time for the presented florescent nanosensor (less than 4 min) can be noticeable.

Comparison of previous reports on gluten detection and the proposed nanosensors (Hnasko et al., 2021; Momeni et al., 2022; Mousavizadeh & Sarlak 2022, 2023; Zhang et al., 2019) also shows similar or some superiority of the nanosensors in terms of linear range, detection limits, speed, selectivity, sensitivity, toxicity of the materials and cost-effectiveness.

Gluten can cause severe allergenic difficulties for younger ages and adults and is becoming more and more prevalent worldwide. For gluten intolerance patients, there is no certain cure unless to follow a severe gluten free diet. Food industries, also should check the gluten content of their foodstuffs and use a “food-allergen” or “gluten-free” labels on their final products. Hence, it has always been critical to find a tool which can raise their diet safety awareness and giving them a chance for a precise and sensitive detection in short period of time, as well as being cost effective, especially in low-income countries.

The detection of gluten in food products depends on whether the foodstuff is processed or not. In recent years, different approaches have been developed to detect gluten in various food products. The most commonly strategy is utilized immunochemical methods. These approaches, apart from their beneficial applications and meeting the criteria of Codex, include some drawbacks like disability of gluten extraction in complex matrices, and their price and shelf-life.

Also, some experimental results showed that the reproducibility of these kits is low. As well, some methods require a pre-treatments stage or sometimes due to interference of antibody binding with the food matrix, the analyzing of gluten in foodstuff becomes challenging and faces difficulties. Furthermore, time and the cost of analysis are items which have to be considered (Yu et al., 2021).

Thus, the development of new analytical methods has been considered. Lately, biosensors or immunosensors as novel strategies have proven to be potential analytical alternatives, because of their safety and secure application in healthcare.

In this work, for the first time, a nanosensor is designed for rapid detection of the gluten content of wheat-containing samples. The proposed nanosensor is made of fluorescent nanomaterials and gluten molecular templates that are simultaneously embedded in a silicate matrix. The fluorescent nanomaterial was made of folic acid-carbon dots (FA-CDs) and was a fluorescent probe. The mentioned fluorescent nanomaterial is made from folic acid-carbon dots (FA-CDs) as a fluorescent probe. Further on, molecularly imprinted polymer technology is used to provide a selective response towards gluten molecules. It is concerning that the combination of molecularly imprinted technique with fluorescence detection has improved the selectivity and sensitivity of the newly proposed nanosensor. The final synthesized structure was dispersed in a buffer and was used as an optical kit in the detection of gluten. After the addition of gluten to this solution, the fluorescence of the structure enhanced effectively due to the adsorption of gluten molecules on the templated space in the structure. As this stage, the concentration range of gluten detection was 0.36 to 2.20 µM (LOD 0.10 µM). Finally, the designed optical nanosensor was successfully applied for gluten content determination in different kinds of wheat flour, some bread samples containing gluten and others labeled as gluten-free.

This novel nitrogen-doped optical nanosensor kit introduced here, not only affirms to be a selective and a low toxic device, but also, confirms to be an inexpensive and customer-friendly tool with a short response time for gluten detection. Furthermore, it requires no specific skills or complex instruments and is able to detect small amount of gluten. Therefore, the provided study opens a new path for developing a workable sensing probe that can raise the confident of celiac patients and bring a peace of mind for them.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research explained in the article was financially supported by Iran University of Science and Technology and University of Tehran.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2022.135383.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
